1: The date formula — vayehi bishtei ʿesre shanah beʾeḥad laḥodesh — "in the eleventh year, on the first of the month" — is unusual in omitting the month. Either the month is implied from context or there is a textual gap. The precision of Ezekiel's other date-formulas (Ezekiel 1:1; Ezekiel 8:1; Ezekiel 20:1; Ezekiel 24:1; Ezekiel 29:1; Ezekiel 30:20; Ezekiel 31:1; Ezekiel 32:1, 17; Ezekiel 33:21; Ezekiel 40:1) suggests a month is missing here. The absence has attracted scribal notice since antiquity.
2: yaʿan ʾasher ʾamrah Tsur ʿal-Yerushalaim heʾaḥ nishberah delet haʿammim nasavvu ʾelai ʾemmalleʾ hoḥarebah — "because Tyre said of Jerusalem: Aha! the gate of the peoples is broken; it has turned to me; I shall be replenished, now that she is laid waste" — delet haʿammim (gate of the peoples) refers to Jerusalem's strategic commercial role; Tyre's gloating (heʾaḥ, the exclamation of malicious delight) is the same that condemns Ammon in Ezekiel 25:3. The verb nasavvu ʾelai (turned to me) reflects Tyre's expectation of absorbing Jerusalem's trade.
3: YHWH's hostile-presence formula — hinneni ʿaleikha Tsur — introduces the oracle. The metaphor of waves (kammayyim ʿolim goyim, nations rising like the sea) applied to the attacking nations is appropriate given Tyre's maritime identity. The sea that is Tyre's strength becomes the vehicle of its destruction.
4: The stripping of Tyre's walls and towers — venatats ḥomot Tsur uheharset migdaleha — uses the verbs natas (pull down) and haras (demolish), the standard terminology for city-destruction throughout the prophets. The result — veḥisoti ʿafarah mimennah venatati ʾotah letsuaḥ sela (I will scrape her dust from her and make her a bare rock) — introduces the key pun: tsur (rock) is both the Hebrew name for Tyre and the common noun for rock. Tyre will become what her name means.
5: The bare-rock image is developed — mishtoaḥ ḥaramim tihyeh betokh hayam — "she will be a place for spreading nets in the midst of the sea." The image of a fishing net-drying rock in the sea emphasizes Tyre's reduction from an empire to a substrate. The declaration ʾani dibbartim neʾum ʾAdanai YHWH (I have spoken, declares Lord YHWH) invokes divine authority as the guarantee of fulfillment.
6: The satellite settlements on the mainland — benoteyha ʾasher bassadeh (her daughters in the field) — are the mainland towns around Tyre proper. They will fall by the sword (baḥerev tiharagna) — the same fate as the island city but by an earthly army rather than the sea-imagery used for Tyre herself.
7: melek hamelakhim — "king of kings" — is Nebuchadnezzar's title in Ezekiel (also Daniel 2:37; Ezra 7:12). The Aramaic and Hebrew titles translate Akkadian šar šarrāni, the imperial honorific of the Babylonian and Assyrian kings. The listing of his military assets (sus verekev vefarashim, horses, chariots, horsemen + infantry with shields, helmets, and spears) is the standard battle-array formula. He comes from the north (mittsfon) — the compass direction of Babylon's approach to Canaan via the Fertile Crescent.
8: The siege procedure — bnot shefelekha baḥerev yaharog venadhan ʿaleikha dayeq ushapal ʿaleikha solelah — siege-ramp (solelah), siege-wall (dayeq), shield-roof (tsinnah) — is the full technical vocabulary of ancient siege warfare. The same sequence appears in siege descriptions in 4:2 and Ezekiel 17:17.
9: The battering ram against the walls — umḥi qabbelet yittein beḥomotayikh vemigreynotav yittots migdaleikha — qabbelet (battering engine) from qabal (to be opposite/facing); migreynotav (his axes/picks) or possibly "battle-axes." Both terms are military technical vocabulary that preserves the specific ancient engineering of siege assault.
10: The abundance of Nebuchadnezzar's horses (merov susav yekhasseikh ʿafaram) emphasizes the numerical superiority of the invaders — their dust alone will cover the city. The noise of horsemen and wagon-wheels and chariots entering the gates (bebôʾô bivaʿayikh kevaʾu befaret ʿir meforatsah) gives the imagery of breach: he enters through the shattered gates as through a breached city.
11: befarsat susav yirmessek — "with the hooves of his horses he will trample all your streets" — the personalization of the horses' hooves on Tyre's streets intensifies the violence. The killing of the people (yotsatsekha ʾet-ʿammekha baḥerev yaharog) and the toppling of the steles (vematsebot ʿuzzekha laʾarets tirad) — the memorial pillars of Tyre — represent both demographic and religious destruction.
12: The plundering sequence — veshalelu ḥeileikh (they will plunder your wealth), webazezu rekhullateikh (loot your merchandise), veharshu ḥomotayikh (pull down your walls), venyitstsetshu batei ḥemudateikh (demolish your fine houses) — and the disposal of the rubble and timber into the sea (vehishlikhuh ʾel-tokh hayam) is the specific prediction that later commentators noted as pointing to Alexander's causeway-construction from the rubble.
13: The silencing of Tyre's music — vehishbatti hamon shirayikh veqol kinnoroteikh loʾ yishamaʿ ʿod — "I will put an end to the noise of your songs and the sound of your lyres shall be heard no more" — uses the same silencing vocabulary as the anti-Babylon oracles in Revelation 18:22. The music of the commercial city is a marker of its prosperity; its silence marks desolation.
14: The bare-rock conclusion returns — venatattikh letsuaḥ sela mishtoaḥ ḥaramim tihyi loʾ tibbane ʿod — "I will make you a bare rock; you will be a place for spreading nets; you will never be rebuilt" — the loʾ tibbane ʿod (never be rebuilt) is the absolute form of the desolation oracle, used also for Babylon (Isaiah 13:20) and Nineveh (Nahum 1:14). The divine self-declaration follows: ki ʾani YHWH dibbartim.
15: The reaction of the coastlands to Tyre's fall — hakoh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH leTs veharaʿash mishmia ḥallaleikh beʾanqot ḥalal beharag herag betokhakh — the trembling of the coastlands at the sound of Tyre's fall (raʿash) anticipates the extended mourning-lament of Ezekiel 27. The structural pattern of Ezekiel 26 is: announcement (vv1-6) → battle description (vv7-14) → cosmic reaction (vv15-18) → divine verdict (vv19-21).
16: The princes of the sea (kol nesiʾei hayam) descending from their thrones, removing their robes, and sitting on the ground in mourning garments (ʿal-haʾarets yeshvu uḥaradu leragatim veshamehu ʿaleikh) — the gestures of mourning (sitting on the ground, trembling, horror) are the standard mourning postures of the ANE. Their response is the emotional correlate of Tyre's fall's economic-political significance.
17: The qinah (lament) the maritime princes compose — venasʾu ʿaleikh qinah veʾamru lakh — begins here and continues through v18. The rhythmic 3+2 qinah meter (the standard Hebrew lament meter of Lamentations) shapes the verse. The content — eikh ʾabaddt nashevet miyammim haʿir hahulala (how you have perished, you who were inhabited from the seas, the praised city) — uses the rhetorical eikh (how!) that opens Lamentations 1:1.
18: The fear of the coastlands on the day of Tyre's fall — ʿattah yeḥardetu ha-iyyim beyom mappaltek venivhalu hayyim ʾasher bayam mitsʾeteikh — "now the islands will tremble on the day of your fall; the islands in the sea are terrified by your passing" — the two verbs yiḥradu (tremble) and nivhalu (are terrified) express the psychic shock of the whole maritime world at the removal of Tyre from it.
19: The deep-water imagery of the divine judgment — ki koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH betiiti ʾoteikh kʿarim neʾeshamot kahaʿalot aleikh ʾet-tehom umikhasseikh hayyim harabim ʿaleikh — YHWH bringing up the deep (tehom) over Tyre returns to the creation-chaos vocabulary. Tehom is the primordial deep of Genesis 1:2; covering a city with it inverts creation and reduces a human settlement to primordial chaos.
20: Descent to the pit — vehoredetikh ʾet-yordei vor ʾel-ʿam ʿolam — "I will bring you down with those who go to the pit, to the ancient people" — bor (pit/cistern) is one of the Hebrew terms for Sheol; ʿam ʿolam (ancient people) refers to the long-dead of previous generations. The final state of Tyre is existence among the dead, not among the living, in the land (betsiyot taḥtiyot kaḥarevot ʿolam, the desolate places of the deep, as in ancient ruins).
21: The terror-term conclusion — ballahot ʾettenek veʾeynek loʾ tihyeh uvuvuqesh uloʾ timmatsʾi leʿolam ʿod — "I will make you a terror and you will cease to be; though you are sought, you will never be found again." Ballahot (terrors/sudden destruction) is a term used in Job (Ezekiel 18:11, 14) for the terrors that pursue the wicked. The complete cessation — never to be found — is the eschatological form of the desolation oracle.
